description: ln -s does not use -n flag, causing incorrect behavior when a stale symlink (pointing to wrong target) already exists. On macOS/Linux, ln -s follows an existing symlink-to-directory and creates the new symlink INSIDE that directory instead of replacing the stale symlink. The fix is to use ln -snf to atomically replace stale symlinks.

The results of execute_command for ln -s (symlink creation) and mkdir -p (parent directory creation) are not checked for success. If the commands fail (e.g., permission denied, disk full), the failure is silently ignored and the user gets no indication that their share path was not linked.
